---
title: "We made a map of the doom debate. Here's where everyone actually stands."
date: "June 2026"
author:
  - Sean Herrington
  - Khai Tran
  - David Bravo
  - Kevin Caldwell
  - Paul Hindoian
  - Josh Tuffy
  - Mikaela Cankosyan
  - Christopher A. Davis
  - Maryam Hampaei
---

*This is the third post in a series titled "We made a map of the doom debate," produced as part of the AI Safety Camp 2026 "Assumptions of the Doom Debate" project, led by Sean Herrington. The [first post](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/dZZD5Lviid2mALAZL/we-made-a-map-of-the-doom-debate) introduced the tree; the [second](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/AZrMGwBPMJmAcgqX8/we-made-a-map-of-the-doom-debate-here-s-how-the-breakdown) walked its nodes. This post places the people. The interactive tree is [here](https://lifeuniversesafety.com/doom-assumptions/index.html).*

TL;DR:

- The AI-risk debate tangles two kinds of question: empirical ones, which evidence can settle, and framing choices, which it never will. Most "disagreements about how dangerous AI is" are really the second kind in disguise.
- We place the recurring figures (Yudkowsky, Bengio, LeCun, Christiano, Critch, and others) on the tree from the first two posts, by where their reasoning actually lives.
- Laid out this way, the sharp disagreements turn out narrow, often a single crux, and the apparent alliances thin, often nothing but a shared conclusion.

::: {.callout-note .meta-note collapse="true" title="Editor note (v2, tree-organized)"}
Working version of Post 3, themed to match Posts 1–2: the gallery walks the tree top-down (Non-AI, then AI-Driven and its branches) using the site's node names. Framing and cruxes sit around it. Kept alongside the flat-list draft until we settle.
:::

---

## Why this map exists

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** States the thesis (two kinds of question) and hands off. Withholds the diagnostic and the named axes for the next section so the two don't double-explain.
:::

The AI debate is confused, and people argue past each other. Two researchers can report the same probability of catastrophe and hold opposite pictures of the world; two who sit forty points apart can hold nearly the same one. A decade of shared evidence has moved almost no one toward almost anyone. That is the clue worth chasing. When evidence reliably fails to move a disagreement, the disagreement was probably never about evidence.

Two kinds of question are knotted together in every estimate, and they behave nothing alike. Some are empirical. Does power-seeking come with capability? Would a failure be visible before it was terminal? Evidence speaks to these, and given time it settles them. The others are not empirical at all. Does an AI that quietly succeeds us count as catastrophe, or as worthy heirs? Over what timeline are we even asking? Would a well-behaved model lower your fear, or is that exactly what a dangerous one would show you? No experiment settles those, because they decide what the experiments mean.

The first two posts built a tree to keep this straight, breaking the debate into the cruxes that carry the real decision weight. This post puts people on the tree. It takes the names that keep coming up, Yudkowsky, Bengio, LeCun, Christiano, Critch, and others, sets each where their reasoning actually lives, and finds the live disagreements are narrower and stranger than the shouting suggests: people who share a conclusion and nothing else, people who agree on everything but one crux.

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/framing.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/intro.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/e-distributional.qmd >}}

## II. AI-Driven Pathway

The worlds where AI raises the danger. They take two shapes, depending on whether the danger runs through many systems at once or through a single dominant one.

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/b-distributed.qmd >}}

### II.2 Single dominant AI pathway

One system carries most of the danger. It splits by whether that system has an internal model of what it is doing, and, if it does, whether it expects the catastrophe it causes.

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/a-knowing-optimizer.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/c-the-mistake.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/d-unwitting.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/adjacencies.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/cuts.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/conclusion.qmd >}}
